data.title: Strong Macau traffic during Chinese New Year bodes well for casino revenue
data.description:
Early reports out of Macau on the impact of the Chinese New Year holiday are generally positive. So far, the holiday is seeing record peak‑day visitor traffic but slightly softer cumulative arrivals in comparison to last year. The assessment of analysts is that Macau casino operators are tracking toward posting solid holiday gaming results, perhaps even ahead of expectations.

According to tourism officials, Macau welcomed 551,623 visitors over the first four days of the Chinese New Year holiday, averaging about 138,000 arrivals per day. That total is roughly 8.4% below the same period in 2025, though officials emphasize that daily traffic has consistently exceeded the 100,000‑visitor level widely cited by local businesses as a profitability threshold. Notably, Macau set a new all‑time single‑day record with around 227,943 visitor arrivals and more than 750,000 cross‑boundary movements in total. In terms of casino floor traffic, GGR Asia highlighted moderate levels on day 6 of the 9-day Chinese New Year holiday.

Looking ahead, analysts are generally calling for combined January–February gross gaming revenue to rise roughly 13% year over year after adjusting for the timing of the festival. Growth is expected for both the premium‑mass and mass segments, while the VIP segment is forecast to lag slightly.

Macau-related casino stocks: Wynn Macau (WYNMF) (WYNMY), Wynn Resorts (WYNN), Sands China (SCHYY) (SCHYF), Las Vegas Sands (LVS), MGM China (MCHVF) (MCHVY), MGM Resorts (MGM), Galaxy Entertainment (GXYEF), SJM Holdings (SJMHF) (SJMHY), Melco Resorts & Entertainment (MLCO), and Studio City International (MSC).

data.title: Hamilton signals measured growth with 55% attritional loss ratio guidance amid competitive market
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Hamilton Insurance Group, Ltd. (HG) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Giuseppina Albo, CEO, highlighted a period of significant growth since the company's IPO, stating "we've delivered consistently strong results, results that allowed tangible book value per share to grow 67% since the IPO." She emphasized the company's technical underwriting approach and strong client relationships. Albo reported record net income for 2025 of $577 million and a combined ratio of 92.9%, with gross premiums written up 21% to $2.9 billion. She attributed performance to "skilled risk selection, commitment to cycle management and strong broker and client relationships."
  • CEO Albo described a transitioning market environment: "After several hard market years, we now find ourselves in a transitioning market, but importantly, one that still provides ample pockets of attractive opportunities for underwriters like ours who are technical, astute and nimble." She noted that growth going forward "will be more measured than it was in the past."
  • CFO Craig Howie stated, "In 2025, Hamilton had a very strong year of financial results with record net income of $577 million, 44% above the $400 million of net income in 2024." He also highlighted the Bermuda substance-based tax credit as a new benefit, recording a $20.7 million credit in 2025, and announced a special dividend of $2 per common share, with an aggregate payment of approximately $206 million. Howie noted ongoing share repurchases totaling $93 million for the year.

Outlook

  • Management expects the market in 2026 to "remain competitive, but that pricing across the lines of business that we target to remain largely risk adequate." Albo remarked, "while we are confident in our ability to continue to find attractive opportunities, I expect our growth going forward to be more measured than it was in the past."
  • CFO Howie provided guidance for 2026, stating, "we are increasing our catastrophe and headline loss threshold from the current $5 million to $10 million... We would expect the attritional loss ratio to run at about 55% in 2026." He also projected a Bermuda credit of about $27 million for 2026.
  • Corporate expenses are expected to "run between $45 million and $50 million for the year."

Financial Results

  • Hamilton delivered net income of $172 million for Q4 2025, equal to $1.69 per diluted share, with operating income of $168 million, or $1.65 per diluted share. The annualized return on average equity for the quarter was 25%.
  • Underwriting income for the quarter was $76 million, with a group combined ratio of 87.0%. The loss ratio improved to 54.6%, down from 60.1% in the prior period, mainly due to lower net catastrophe losses.
  • Gross premiums written for the quarter increased 23%. Bermuda segment grew 27% in Q4, driven by casualty reinsurance, while International segment premiums increased 20%. Hamilton Select grew 19% during the quarter, focusing on casualty classes.
  • Investment income for Q4 2025 was $98 million. The Two Sigma Hamilton Fund produced a $56 million net return for the quarter; for the year, a net return of 16.0% or $301 million.
  • Shareholders' equity at year-end was $2.8 billion, book value per share was $28.50.

Q&A

  • Hristian Getsov, Wells Fargo: Asked about the underlying loss ratio guide and the decision behind the special dividend. CFO Howie explained the 55% guidance is "the change in our threshold from the $5 million to the $10 million threshold is what's taking that loss pick up to the 55% guidance." On the dividend, Howie said, "A special dividend is an active and an effective way of returning that capital quickly to our shareholders. But we will also be able to continue buying back shares."
  • Getsov also questioned competition in the E&S Select platform. Albo responded that growth was "completely in line with what our expectations were," noting competition is seen on professional lines, not casualty.
  • Thomas Mcjoynt-Griffith, KBW: Asked about premium leverage and data center opportunities. Howie said premium leverage "hasn't really changed very much" and remains at about 80%. Albo said, "we are also a little bit cautious here on accumulation... particularly on the large data centers."
  • Daniel Cohen, BMO: Inquired about reserve developments. Howie stated, "overall, our reserves were favorable for the quarter. None of that came from casualty."
  • Chongwook Lee, Barclays: Asked for returns from the Two Sigma Hamilton fund and about market dynamics in property and casualty. Howie said reporting will now be quarterly with no lag. Albo noted, "On the casualty side, we are still seeing some healthy increases on the insurance side of the equation."
  • David Samar, Citizens: Questioned the source of funds for the special dividend and large losses. Howie stated it is from "available cash on hand as well as the fixed income portfolio," and explained the largest loss was a satellite loss.
  • Matthew Heimermann, Citi: Asked about investing Bermuda tax credit savings and technology. Howie said, "we're going to continue to invest in our projects, our operations and our technology to operate at scale in Bermuda." Albo cited ongoing investment in underwriting and technology, including AI.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a neutral to slightly positive tone, focusing on technical questions regarding loss ratio guidance, capital management decisions, and competitive dynamics. Questions sought clarity on guidance changes and the company’s strategic positioning.
  • Management's sentiment was confident and disciplined in prepared remarks, with Albo stating, "we are confident in our ability to continue to find attractive opportunities." During Q&A, responses remained composed and explanatory, reflecting a commitment to transparency and measured growth. Compared to the previous quarter, the tone was slightly more measured regarding future growth, but confidence in technical execution and capital discipline persisted.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted to emphasize more measured growth in 2026, with explicit commentary on maintaining discipline and not chasing top-line growth at the expense of margins.
  • The attritional loss ratio guidance for 2026 was raised to 55%, influenced by a change in the catastrophe and headline loss threshold, compared to the prior quarter's focus around 54.4%.
  • Management continued to highlight strong capital position, but introduced a special dividend and updated expectations for the Bermuda tax credit.
  • Analysts continued to focus on underwriting results, capital management, and competitive positioning, mirroring themes from the previous quarter but probing new guidance and changes in business mix.
  • The tone from management remained confident, but with greater emphasis on market competition and the need for disciplined, selective growth.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged a transitioning market with increasing competition, particularly in property and professional lines, and indicated selective underwriting as a mitigation strategy.
  • Elevated large losses were noted for the quarter, with CFO Howie citing a satellite loss as the largest event, prompting changes in loss threshold reporting.
  • The company remains cautious on emerging opportunities such as data centers, monitoring accumulation risk and avoiding extensive exposure to business interruption claims.
  • Analysts raised concerns about the sustainability of reserve favorability, capital deployment, and the impact of market competition on future growth and margins.

Final Takeaway

Hamilton Insurance Group closed 2025 with record financial results, strong underwriting and investment performance, and a robust capital position. Looking ahead, management signaled a disciplined approach to growth in a more competitive market, guiding to a 55% attritional loss ratio and prioritizing sustainable profitability over top-line expansion. The company’s capital management strategy includes both a special dividend and ongoing share repurchases, reflecting confidence in its balance sheet and operational resilience while remaining vigilant to market risks and selective in new business opportunities.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Top Oil And Gas Refining and Marketing Stocks by Year-To-Date Performance
data.description:
Year to date, the energy sector has been one of the market’s strongest performers. In light of this, below is a list of the top oil and gas refining and marketing stocks ranked by their Year-To-Date performance. 

The list is topped by Calumet (CLMT), with a YTD performance of 47.91%. Green Plains (GPRE) and Clean Energy Fuels (CLNE) follow as the next top contenders in the sector.

Other major players rounding out the top five include PBF Energy (PBF) and Valero Energy (VLO). The list also features established names like Marathon Petroleum (MPC) and Phillips 66 (PSX), along with Par Pacific (PARR) and Sunoco LP (SUN)

Here is the list:

  1. Calumet, Inc. (CLMT), YTD perf: 47.91%, Quant rating: Buy 4.31

  2. Green Plains Inc. (GPRE), YTD perf: 38.47%, Quant rating: Strong Buy 4.63

  3. Clean Energy Fuels Corp. (CLNE), YTD perf: 25.24%, Quant rating: Sell 2.49

  4. PBF Energy Inc. (PBF), YTD perf: 25.00%, Quant rating: Buy 3.95

  5. Valero Energy Corporation (VLO), YTD perf: 22.39%, Quant rating: Buy 4.17

  6. Marathon Petroleum Corporation (MPC), YTD perf: 20.81%, Quant rating: Hold 3.15

  7. Phillips 66 (PSX), YTD perf: 20.44%, Quant rating: Hold 3.14

  8. Par Pacific Holdings, Inc. (PARR), YTD perf: 20.40%, Quant rating: Hold 3.15

  9. SunocoCorp LLC (SUNC), YTD perf: 17.88%, Quant rating: Not Covered

Energy ETFs: (XLE), (AMLP), (VDE), (XOP), (OIH), and (IXC)

data.title: BorgWarner's new venture with TurboCell poses risks not yet priced in -- UBS
data.description:
BorgWarner (BWA) is offsetting deceleration in its core business by branching out into data center power generation, an industry poised for explosive growth, and a decision that should translate into meaningful gains in the company’s top-line.

However, BorgWarner (BWA) has yet to provide concrete details on its new opportunity with TurboCell. With much of the potential already reflected in the share price, UBS has adopted a more cautious stance, downgrading the stock to Sell from Neutral.

“To be clear, we applaud BWA for seeking new growth opportunities,” analyst Joseph Spak said in his note to clients. “But we believe the bull case assumes fully scaled $1.5B in sales by 2030…we expect a slower ramp to half that level,” Spak adds, assuming that if even the bull case is correct, 50% of the upside has already been priced in.

Rather than simply moving to the sidelines, Spak believes BorgWarner (BWA) is a sell at current levels, not because of the risks of the power generation business, but instead because of risks specific to the BorgWarner (BWA) and TurboCell partnership.

First, TurboCell is a startup, and BWA has never made turbines at scale. This combo “amplifies the execution risk,” Spak says.

Second, BWA indicated the cost of ownership is compelling. But without pricing or detailed specifications, Spak thinks this is difficult to verify.

And finally, the product is still in development and not yet commercialized. While there is a “built-in” buyer with Edged through TurboCell’s parent Endeavor, Spak is uneasy with the lack of public information on either Endeavor, Edged, or TurboCell.

“Until the product is more proven, bringing on third-party customers could take time,” he says.

With these considerations, as well as underwhelming expectations for BWA’s core automotive business, “the scenarios that investors have to believe to justify upside may prove to be quite optimistic.”

Spak’s Sell rating on BorgWarner (BWA) is a notable outlier among not only Wall Street analysts (Deutsche Bank recently upgraded the stock to a Buy) and Seeking Alpha analysts—all of whom view BorgWarner (BWA) as a Buy—but also Seeking Alpha’s Quant rating, which views BorgWarner (BWA) as a Strong Buy with a Quant score of 4.54 out of 5.

After setting an all-time high last week on Q4 results/TurboCell news, BorgWarner (BWA) shares have retreated, spending the subsequent five consecutive days in the red.

data.title: Cybersecurity stocks fall after Anthropic unveils Claude Code Security
data.description:
Anthropic (ANTHRO) unveiled a new feature called Claude Code Security built into Claude Code on the web.

Cybersecurity stocks were in the red on Friday. CrowdStrike (CRWD) and Cloudflare (NET) each slumped about 6%. Datadog (DDOG) fell around 3%, while Fortinet (FTNT) dipped about 2%.

The company said it is releasing the new capability as a limited research preview to Enterprise and Team customers, with expedited access for maintainers of open-source repositories, so people can work together to refine its capabilities and ensure it is deployed responsibly.  Open-source maintainers need to apply for the free, expedited access.

Anthropic noted that the new feature scans codebases for security vulnerabilities and suggests targeted software patches for human review, allowing teams to find and fix security issues that traditional methods often miss.

The company added that it recently showed that Claude can detect new, high-severity vulnerabilities. But the same capabilities that help defenders find and fix vulnerabilities could help attackers exploit them, the company added.

Anthropic — which is backed by Amazon (AMZN) and Alphabet's (GOOG) (GOOGL) Google —  said Claude Code Security is intended to put this power in the hands of defenders and protect code against this new category of AI-enabled attack. 

Claude Code Security builds on more than a year of research into Claude’s cybersecurity capabilities, the company added.

data.title: Trump calls Supreme Court's tariff ruling 'deeply disappointing'
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump on Friday criticized the Supreme Court's decision striking down his sweeping tariffs, calling the ruling "deeply disappointing."

Speaking at the White House, Trump said he was "absolutely ashamed" of Supreme Court members for "not having the courage to do what's right for our country," hours after SCOTUS ruled 6-3 that he exceeded his authority under an emergency powers law by imposing broad tariffs without clear congressional backing, 

Meanwhile, the president vowed to pursue alternative paths to advance his trade agenda. 

This is a developing story. Check back for updates.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: KKR mulls sale of unit BMC Helix - report
data.description:
KKR & Co. (KKR) is considering a sale of its BMC Helix unit, people familiar with the matter told Reuters.

The New York-based firm has reportedly secured initial bids from private equity firms and corporate buyers.

BMC Helix generates about $150M in EBITDA and ~$750M in annual recurring revenue, the sources reportedly said.

The sale could fetch as much as $1.5B, according to the people, who requested anonymity while discussing non-public matters.

Investment bankers at Jefferies are said to be advising BMC Helix on the sale.

KKR declined to comment on the potential transaction.

data.title: Wall Street moves higher as market participants assess GDP and PCE data
data.description:
Wall Street’s major market averages move higher on Friday as investors react to the PCE report, which came in hotter than expected.

The tech-focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was +0.5%. At the same time, the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was +0.45%, and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) was +0.1%. 

From a sector vantage point, six of the S&P 500’s 11 segments were in positive trading territory, with communication services at the top. Going the other way, energy has struggled the most so far. 

Treasury yields inched higher as the shorter-end U.S. 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) moved up by 2 basis points to 3.48%, and the longer-end U.S. 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) moved up by 2 basis points to 4.09%. 

On the economic calendar, PCE inflation ticked up in December and came in higher than the consensus. The Q4 U.S. GDP (first estimate) came in at +1.4%, annual rate, vs. +2.8% consensus and +4.4% in the prior quarter.

“Friday's GDP for the fourth quarter was much lower-than-expected, but it was still positive and shows that the economy expanded at respectable clip during the final few months of 2025 even as fears about the labor market started to intensify. We expect a strong year of economic growth in 2026, driven by business investment, consumer spending and fading trade headwinds,” Rick Gardner, chief investment officer of RGA Investments noted.

As for stocks that were on the move, shares of Corning Incorporated (GLW) climbed 6.3%, and shares of Intel (INTC) came down by 2.4%.

data.title: Celsius Holdings rallies after strong growth potential update
data.description:
Celsius Holdings (CELH) rallied on Friday after its presentation at the Consumer Analyst Group of New York Conference caught the eye of investors. Notably, the beverage company expects its distribution footprint to continue growing, as the Celsius and Alani Nu brands continue to show momentum. 

Management said it sees a long runway of double‑digit top‑line growth, driven by category tailwinds, broader distribution, and an expanded multi‑brand energy platform anchored by the key PepsiCo (PEP) partnership. Structural shifts by consumers toward zero-sugar, functional products are seen as a tailwind. Consumption trends are also working in Celsius' (CELH) favor, with more consumers using energy drinks across social, meal, and fitness moments.

Looking ahead, convenience stores are considered a significant white‑space opportunity, with c‑stores representing ~60% of energy sales. Celsius (CELH) signaled a significant opportunity to increase its share of space and facings in the channel.Shares of Celsius Holdings (CELH) were up 8.0% in early afternoon trading to reverse a four-week downtrend.

data.title: Employers signals 10% premium growth target for new excess workers’ comp product while advancing AI-driven efficiency
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Employers Holdings, Inc. (EIG) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Katherine Antonello began by addressing the elevated frequency of California cumulative trauma (CT) claims, noting "To be clear, this remains a California-specific issue. Claim frequency in our other states and within non-CT claims in California continues to trend favorably." She emphasized that Employers implemented rate increases and tightened underwriting restrictions in California and other regions, while adding, "these actions... are also likely to reduce written premium in 2026."
  • Antonello highlighted that the Small Commercial segment maintained strong retention rates throughout 2025, attributing this to investments in automation and ease of use. She confirmed that the fourth quarter full actuarial assessment required no additional reserve strengthening, and an independent actuarial firm validated that carried reserves were within reasonable ranges.
  • A new excess workers’ compensation product was introduced, described as "a strategic expansion of our capabilities," with Antonello reporting "the early market response has been strong, and we expect this product will deepen our distribution partner relationships while expanding our addressable market."
  • $215 million was returned to shareholders via share repurchases and dividends in 2025. Antonello stated, "Along with our operational performance, these actions increased our book value per share, including the deferred gain by 11% to $51.31."
  • CFO Michael Pedraja reported, "Gross premiums written were $156.8 million compared to $176.3 million for the prior year quarter, a decrease of 11% due primarily to a decrease in new business writings and lower final audit premiums, partially offset by higher renewal business premium."
  • Pedraja added, "Our losses in LAE were $134.4 million versus $113.2 million a year ago, an increase of 18.7% due primarily to an increase in the accident year 2025 selected loss and LAE ratio and the absence of favorable developments in the fourth quarter of this year."
  • Pedraja detailed the investment portfolio rebalancing, noting, "The investment rebalancing accomplished several goals, including reducing our equity investments to target allocation, increasing our overall investment portfolio yield by a net 40 basis points, extracting an estimated net present value gain of $16 million and reducing our required capital."

Outlook

  • Management expects a reduction in written premium in 2026 as a result of tightened underwriting and pricing actions in California. Antonello noted, "While we're confident that these California pricing and underwriting actions... will strengthen our underwriting profitability, they are also likely to reduce written premium in 2026."
  • The company anticipates further improvement in the expense ratio, driven by ongoing AI initiatives. Antonello said, "We believe it will continue to decline with our enterprise-wide deployment of AI."
  • Employers is targeting the new excess workers’ compensation product to be a "meaningful top line revenue growth driver" over the next several years, aiming for it to constitute "10% of our overall written premium over the next 4 to 7 years."

Financial Results

  • Gross premiums written for the quarter were $156.8 million. Losses and loss adjustment expenses totaled $134.4 million. Net investment income reached $31.4 million, attributed to private equity investment returns and a higher book yield on the fixed income portfolio.
  • After a portfolio rebalancing, the weighted average book yield increased to 4.9%. The after-tax realized loss on fixed income sales was $40 million, reducing net income and adjusted book value per share for the quarter.
  • Adjusted net income, excluding realized/unrealized investment gains and losses and LPT deferred gain amortization, was $14.5 million for the quarter.
  • Employers repurchased almost 2.4 million shares at an average price of $40.94 per share in Q4, and continued buybacks into early 2026.

Q&A

  • Mark Hughes, Truist: Asked about the trajectory of CT claims in California. Antonello responded, "We are seeing throughout 2025 that the acceleration of the frequency that we saw sort of in early '25 and throughout 2024... has flattened quite a bit. So that has been good news."
  • Hughes questioned the competitive dynamic in California. Antonello explained, "I would characterize the California market as hardening. Generally speaking, though, I'd say across the country, the environment is still fairly competitive."
  • Hughes inquired about share buybacks for 2026. Antonello stated, "I do expect it will return to a normal level of repurchase authority in 2026, absent some change, but we're trying to be very opportunistic."
  • Hughes asked about further expense ratio improvement if top line is down. Antonello said, "We are hoping to still get improvement. As I mentioned, we have a lot of AI initiatives that are underway."
  • Karol Chmiel, Citizens: Queried about gross written premium in California. Antonello confirmed, "We're seeing lower new business writings in California, and we've selected some classes of business that we are exiting, not just in California, but countrywide."
  • Chmiel followed up on scaling the new excess workers’ comp product and future offerings. Antonello replied, "We do have other products in mind. We're not quite ready to announce those yet... we think over time that this could be a meaningful top line revenue growth driver for us as a company."
  • Unknown Analyst (Green Capital) probed on competitive advantages and target performance for excess workers’ comp. Antonello responded, "We do feel like we're going to be able to provide quotes in a faster manner because of the AI tool... relative to the guaranteed cost business... the excess comp space, while it's a bit, what I would say, lumpier, overall, we feel like it's going to perform in the mid-80s in terms of a combined ratio."
  • When asked about long-term scale, Antonello said, "We would love to see this be 10% of our overall written premium over the next 4 to 7 years."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts repeatedly pressed on the trajectory of CT claims, competitive positioning, and the scaling of new products, reflecting a slightly cautious tone with a focus on risk management and future growth drivers.
  • Management maintained a confident tone throughout, particularly in prepared remarks. Antonello stated, "We're absolutely confident in the path that we're on." During Q&A, management was deliberate and transparent, but used caveats such as "not ready to claim victory yet" when discussing trends, indicating a measured approach.
  • Compared to the prior quarter, analysts showed continued concern about California CT claims, while management’s confidence appeared more grounded, emphasizing validation of reserves by external actuaries and the flattening of adverse claim trends.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • In Q3, management discussed a significant reserve strengthening due to California CT claims and announced the initial development of the excess workers’ comp product. In Q4, management confirmed no additional reserve strengthening was needed and reported validation from an independent actuary.
  • The Q4 call emphasized execution: product launch for excess workers’ comp (now accepting submissions), expanded AI initiatives, and a completed recapitalization plan. Guidance now signals a likely reduction in written premium due to tighter underwriting and pricing in California, whereas Q3 focused on underwriting margin over growth.
  • Analysts' questions in both quarters focused on CT claim trends, competitive dynamics, and capital deployment, but in Q4, attention shifted toward the scaling and expected impact of the new product.
  • Management’s tone evolved from caution and defensive explanations in Q3 to a more forward-looking, confident stance in Q4.

Risks and Concerns

  • Elevated frequency of California CT claims remains a business risk, though management noted a flattening of the trend.
  • Management acknowledged that tightened underwriting and pricing may reduce written premium in 2026.
  • The after-tax realized loss of $40 million from fixed income sales impacted net income and adjusted book value per share in the quarter.
  • Competitive pressure in California persists, with management choosing to exit certain classes and geographies where pricing margins are too thin.
  • The performance of the new excess workers’ comp product and its integration into the portfolio presents execution risks, though early indications are positive.

Final Takeaway

Employers Holdings, Inc. is executing a disciplined strategy to address California CT claim pressures through targeted pricing, underwriting, and investment in automation, while simultaneously launching a new excess workers’ compensation product that is expected to drive up to 10% of written premium over the next 4 to 7 years. The company’s expense ratio continues to improve, underpinned by enterprise-wide AI adoption, and reserves have been validated by independent actuaries. Management remains confident in its capital position and shareholder return strategy, as evidenced by share repurchases and dividends, while signaling a cautious approach to premium growth in 2026 amid ongoing market challenges in California.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
